epigraph.text: Great are the works of the Lord, studied by all who delight in them.
epigraph.quote: The variety of distributions is a reflection of the variety of phenomena in nature.

content.text:
Several other continuous distributions play important roles in 
statistics.

content.title: Beta Distribution
content.content:
$X$ has a **beta distribution** with 
parameters $\alpha, \beta > 0$, written $X \sim \text{Beta}(\alpha, \beta)$, if:
\[
 f(x) = \frac{\Gamma(\alpha + \beta)}{\Gamma(\alpha)\Gamma(\beta)} 
 x^{\alpha-1}(1-x)^{\beta-1}, \quad 0 < x < 1
\]

content.text:
The beta distribution is defined on $(0, 1)$, making it ideal for 
modeling probabilities and proportions.

content.title: Chi-Square Distribution
content.content:
The **chi-square distribution** 
with $k$ degrees of freedom, written $X \sim \chi^2_k$, is:
\[
 \chi^2_k = \Gamma\left(\frac{k}{2}, \frac{1}{2}\right)
\]

content.text:
If $Z_1, \ldots, Z_k$ are independent standard normal, then:
\[
 Z_1^2 + Z_2^2 + \cdots + Z_k^2 \sim \chi^2_k
\]

content.title: Student's $t$ Distribution
content.content:
The **$t$ distribution** 
with $\nu$ degrees of freedom arises as:
\[
 T = \frac{Z}{\sqrt{V/\nu}}
\]
where $Z \sim \Normal(0,1)$ and $V \sim \chi^2_\nu$ are independent.

content.text:
The $t$ distribution is symmetric and bell-shaped like the normal, 
but with heavier tails. As $\nu \to \infty$, $t_\nu \to \Normal(0,1)$.

content.title: William Sealy Gosset (1876--1937)
content.content:
"Student" was the pseudonym of William Gosset, a chemist at the 
Guinness Brewery in Dublin. Working with small samples of barley 
and hops, he developed the $t$ distribution to account for the 
extra uncertainty when estimating variance from limited data. 
Guinness's secrecy policy required the pseudonym.
